questions.question: Which repository name will GitHub Pages serve at `https://your-username.github.io/` (the apex of the github.io hostname) rather than under a subpath?
questions.explanation: Only a repository named exactly `<username>.github.io` is treated as a user site and served at the apex of that hostname. Any other name is a project site served under `/<repo>/`.

questions.question: You move your blog into a repo called `my-notes` to be served at `https://your-username.github.io/my-notes/`. CSS and links 404 after deploy. What is the single most likely fix in `_config.yml`?
choices: Set `baseurl: "/my-notes"` and pipe paths through the `relative_url` filter in templates
questions.explanation: Project sites serve under a path. `baseurl: "/my-notes"` tells Jekyll's `relative_url`/`absolute_url` filters to prepend that path. Hardcoded `/assets/...` links bypass the filter and 404. There is no `prefix` or `pages` key in `_config.yml`.

questions.question: You can deploy from a branch (built-in Jekyll build) or from GitHub Actions (your own workflow). For a blog whose Module 4 setup uses `jekyll-picture-tag`, which path is the better starting point and why?
choices: Deploy from a branch, because the built-in build is faster
Deploy from a branch, because Actions cannot publish to a user site
Deploy from Actions, because the built-in build's `--safe` mode won't load `jekyll-picture-tag` and the feature will silently disappear in production
Deploy from `/docs`, because Actions doesn't support custom domains
questions.explanation: The built-in build runs `--safe` and only loads safelisted plugins; `jekyll-picture-tag` isn't on the list and is silently dropped. Actions runs your `Gemfile` as written, so the plugin works. Both paths support user sites and custom domains.
